edges.description: Loss of PTCH1 releases SMO from tonic inhibition
Activating SMO mutations bypass PTCH1 inhibition
SMO activation leads to processing and nuclear translocation of GLI1/2
GLI target genes drive cell cycle entry and tumor growth

nodes.description: Loss of PTCH1 or activating SMO mutations result in constitutive activation of the Hedgehog signaling cascade. Active SMO translocates to the primary cilium and promotes activation of GLI transcription factors (GLI1, GLI2), which drive expression of target genes promoting proliferation and survival.

nodes.description: Active Hedgehog signaling promotes GLI transcription factor processing and nuclear translocation. GLI1 and GLI2 activate transcription of target genes including PTCH1, GLI1 (positive feedback), cyclin D1, MYC, and BCL2, promoting proliferation and survival of basal cells.

PTCH1 (Patched 1) encodes a transmembrane receptor that normally suppresses Smoothened (SMO) in the absence of Hedgehog ligand. Loss-of-function mutations in PTCH1 release SMO from inhibition, resulting in constitutive Hedgehog pathway activation. PTCH1 mutations occur in approximately 70% of sporadic BCCs and are the germline cause of Gorlin syndrome.

nodes.description: Smoothened (SMO) is a G-protein coupled receptor-like protein that transduces Hedgehog signal. Activating mutations in SMO, though less common than PTCH1 loss, render SMO constitutively active regardless of PTCH1 status. Some SMO mutations confer resistance to vismodegib.

Constitutive Hedgehog signaling drives proliferation of basal cells through GLI-mediated transcription of cell cycle regulators, leading to tumor formation. The proliferating cells maintain a basal cell phenotype with peripheral palisading characteristic of BCC histology.
